description: Rules requiring sentence-level syntactic analysis: clause types and embeddings, reported speech, focus/cleft constructions, and question forms. Detected via dependency arcs and structural patterns.

examples:
The concert will be in Canecão. Let's go!!! (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Portuguese; Pass)

Let's go to the town centre, it is an interesting place. (China; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2001; Chinese; Pass)

Let's mee
structures.explanation: 'Let's' suggestion — 'let's + base verb' to propose doing something together (e.g. 'Let's go for a walk').

examples:
In the morning, I go to the beach. (Argentina; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

In my country, the most popular food is Paella, which is a kind of rice with seafood, really tasty. (
structures.explanation: Focus / emphasis — Prepositional phrases, fronting.

examples:
It is 3 hours long, and if you want to pass it you will have to study very hard, it is very difficult. (Italy; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2006; Italian; Pass)

If you buy a gift for me, please buy a book, because I
structures.explanation: Real conditional — 'if' + present simple in the condition clause, describing a genuine possibility (e.g. 'If you come early, we can chat').

examples:
The team that I like is called 'The Monkeys', but Paty prefers 'The Walls'. (Uruguay; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

I bought some clothes that I will need for my trip to Greece.
structures.explanation: Defining relative clause with 'that' (object) — 'that' introduces a clause that identifies the noun, which is the object of the relative clause (e.g. 'the film that I watched').

examples:
I came to your house yesterday with the computer game which I borrowed from Mayank. (India; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Hindi; Pass)

The coffee-cup which my good friend gave me is pretty. (China; A2 WAYSTAGE;
structures.explanation: Defining relative clause with 'which' (object) — 'which' identifies a thing that is the object of the relative clause (e.g. 'the car which I bought').

examples:
It's about a girl who went to the war when she was 22 years old. (Mexico; A2 WAYSTAGE; 1999; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

I'd like a pen-friend who really likes music and reading. (China; A2 WAYST
structures.explanation: Defining relative clause with 'who' (subject) — 'who' introduces a clause identifying the person by what they do (e.g. 'the woman who called', 'the man who lives next door').

examples:
I haven't eaten Italian food for a long time. (Ecuador; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

… now I haven't got any money. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Spanish - European; Pass)

I bough
structures.explanation: Negative 'have' — 'don't/doesn't/didn't have' stating the absence of something (e.g. 'I don't have any money', 'She didn't have time').

structures.guideword: FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'SAY', PRONOUN SHIFT
examples:
My mother said that she can take us there. (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Portuguese; Pass)

The teacher said that we have to bring a notebook, a pencil and a rubber. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2006; Spanish -
structures.explanation: Reported speech with 'say' — converting direct speech to indirect using 'say', with tense backshift and pronoun changes (e.g. 'He said he was tired').

structures.guideword: FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'TELL', PRONOUN SHIFT
examples: [about a mobile phone] It's very important to me to have it with me because my brother is in the hospital and my father told me that he will call. (Argentina; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Spanish - Latin Americ
structures.explanation: Reported speech with 'tell' — converting direct speech using 'tell + person', with tense and pronoun backshift (e.g. 'She told me she would come').

examples:
Do write back and let me know all your news. (Spain; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Spanish - European; Pass)

Do let me know your views on this. (India; B1 THRESHOLD; 2004; Others; Pass)

Do follow the instruct
structures.explanation: Emphatic 'do' — auxiliary 'do/does/did' in an affirmative clause to add emphasis or contradict a prior statement (e.g. 'I do like your idea', 'She did warn us').

examples:
It's great that you have got a new job! (Spain; B1 THRESHOLD; 2009; Spanish - European; Pass)

It's true that there are one or two programmes that are interesting or funny, but the rest... they're hor
structures.explanation: 'It is + adjective + that' — structure emphasising the content of the 'that' clause (e.g. 'It is important that you attend', 'It is clear that she knows').

examples:
Let me tell you my story. (Taiwan; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Chinese; Pass)

Let me explain something. (Russia; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Russian; Pass)

Let me answer your question. (Libya; B1 THRESHOLD; 2009; A
structures.explanation: 'Let me' + focus — 'let me + verb' to introduce an offer, clarification, or example (e.g. 'Let me explain', 'Let me show you').

examples: Use the stairs rather than the lift, the bike rather than the tube and whenever you've got enough time, try running around the neighbourhood in the morning before going to work. (Colombia; B1 THRESHOL
structures.explanation: 'Rather than' contrast — shows preference or contrast between two options (e.g. 'She studied rather than going out').

examples:
I think I am going to pay by cash, unless you accept credit cards. (B1 THRESHOLD; 2003; Pass)

If I were you I'd go to work on foot, unless it's far away from your house. (Libya; B1 THRESHOLD; 2009; A
structures.explanation: 'Unless' condition — negative conditional meaning 'except if' (e.g. 'I'll go unless it rains', 'Don't call unless it's urgent').

examples:
The day when he had to go back to Korea, I could not stop myself crying. (Korea, South; B1 THRESHOLD; 1999; Korean; Pass)

I really remember the day when I lost my suitcase in the airport. (Mexico; B1
structures.explanation: 'When' clause — temporal or focusing clause introduced by 'when' (e.g. 'When I was young, I loved climbing').

examples:
How exciting! (China; B1 THRESHOLD; 2003; Chinese; Pass)

How terrible! (Bosnia-Herzegovina; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Serbian; Pass)

How amazing! This is my first flight in a hot-air balloon. (China; B1 T
structures.explanation: 'How + adjective' — exclamatory or embedded question structure (e.g. 'How strange!', 'I know how difficult it is').

examples:
First of all, I think you are too young to go on holiday by yourself. (Cyprus; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Greek; Pass)

He always told me that he was too busy to write a letter to me. (France; B1 THRESHOLD;
structures.explanation: 'Too + to-infinitive' — 'too + adjective/adverb + to + verb' expresses that something is excessive and prevents an action (e.g. 'It's too heavy to lift').

structures.guideword: FORM: SENSE VERBS + 'AS IF' OR 'AS THOUGH' + FINITE CLAUSE
examples:
I was excited and I felt as if I was a bird. (Japan; B1 THRESHOLD; 2003; Japanese; Pass)

So when you enter the restaurant you feel as if you are in Italy. (Italy; B1 THRESHOLD; 2005; German; Pass)

I
structures.explanation: Sense verb + 'as if'/'as though' — linking verb followed by 'as if' or 'as though' to describe an impression (e.g. 'It sounds as if something is wrong').

examples:
The city where I live is very nice and extremely charming. (B1 THRESHOLD; 2003; ; Pass)

But if they are going to a place where you go every year you should go with your friends, you will have more fu
structures.explanation: Relative clause with 'where' — 'where' modifies a place noun (e.g. 'the city where I grew up', 'the office where she works').

examples:
We have a new employee whose name is Mary Watts. (Spain; B1 THRESHOLD; 2002; Spanish - European; Pass)

It was a very famous football player, whose name was known all over the world. (Russia; B1 THRES
structures.explanation: Relative clause with 'whose' — 'whose' introduces a clause showing possession or close relationship (e.g. 'the woman whose bag was stolen', 'the doctor whose name I forgot').

examples:
Let's not lose track of each other again! (Brazil; B2 VANTAGE; 2004; Portuguese; Pass)

Let's not forget that zoos actually help us keep dying species alive …. (B2 VANTAGE; 1993; Swedish; Pass)

Let's
structures.explanation: 'Let's not' — negative suggestion proposing to avoid doing something together (e.g. 'Let's not argue about this').

examples:
In my opinion young people at that age are not mature enough to decide by themselves what they want for their future. (B2 VANTAGE; 2009; German; Pass)

I think I am responsible enough to handle this j
structures.explanation: 'Enough + to-infinitive' — 'adjective + enough + to' expresses that the degree is sufficient for an action (e.g. 'He is old enough to drive', 'Is it warm enough to swim?').

examples:
What we need is a strong publicity campaign, directed at young people. (Portugal; C1 EFFECTIVE OPERATIONAL PROFICIENCY; 1993; Portuguese; Pass)

What everyone loved were the special effects and we wer
structures.explanation: 'What' cleft clause — 'What + clause + is/was + focus' puts special emphasis on a part of the message (e.g. 'What I need is a rest', 'What surprised me was her reaction').

examples:
That night Kevin did not sleep; nor did Kristin. (Spain; C2 MASTERY; 1999; Catalan; Pass)

They do not have any hobbies, nor do they have friends or relatives (at least not where they live). (Sweden;
structures.explanation: Negative + 'nor' — 'nor' following a negative clause for rhetorical emphasis or to add a further negative (e.g. 'I didn't like it, nor did she').

examples: I was my parents' first and only child, and I was brought up at my grandparents' home, because, although married, my mother decided not to leave her parents' home, and my father agreed with her. (Spa
structures.explanation: Non-finite concessive — 'although'/'though' followed by a non-finite clause; formal and condensed concession (e.g. 'Although tired, she kept going').

examples:
She closed her eyes as if to push those thoughts away. (Brazil; C2 MASTERY; 1993; Portuguese; Pass)

There it was: the chef's body, hacked to pieces, a carving knife in his hand as if to scare off an
structures.explanation: 'As if' + non-finite — 'as if' followed by an infinitive or participle clause to describe an appearance or impression (e.g. 'She looked as if about to cry').
Yes/no question with 'be' — question formed by moving 'is/are/was/were' before the subject (e.g. 'Is she coming?', 'Were they late?').

examples:
I've just watched a football competition with my family and friends. Have you watched it? (Japan; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Japanese; Pass)

I left my mobile in your house, have you seen it? (Argentina; A2 W
structures.explanation: Question with 'have' — question using 'have/has/had' in the perfect aspect (e.g. 'Have you seen it?', 'Has she arrived?').

examples:
You don't know where I live, do you? (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Spanish - European; Pass)

It differs from the story which you told me, doesn't it? (Japan; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2010; Japanese; Pass)

It's fan
structures.explanation: Question tag — short question added at the end of a statement to seek confirmation or agreement (e.g. 'It's nice, isn't it?', 'You know him, don't you?').

examples:
What do you think? (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2001; Portuguese; Pass)

What did you do yesterday? (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Spanish - European; Pass)

Where did you go? (Taiwan; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Chinese
structures.explanation: Question with 'do' — question formed using 'do/does/did' as auxiliary (e.g. 'Do you know her?', 'Did they leave?').
